one_line_takeaway: TS-Guard, a multi-task GRPO-trained guardrail that predicts request harmfulness, attack correlation, and a step-level safety label together, paired with TS-Flow's feedback-instead-of-abort integration, cuts attack success rates on AgentDojo/ASB/AgentHarm to single digits while raising (not just preserving) benign task utility relative to both an undefended ReAct agent and a detect-and-abort LlamaFirewall baseline.
key_finding: On TS-Bench strict-mode detection (Table 3), TS-Guard beats every baseline on all three datasets: AgentHarm-Traj ACC/F1/Recall = 84.81/90.16/96.95 (vs. GPT-4o 75.23/84.80/96.19); ASB-Traj = 94.97/94.76/93.85 (vs. GPT-4o 65.84/63.03/60.11, the next-best on this split); AgentDojo-Traj = 91.72/86.18/89.49 (vs. GPT-4o 55.74/56.59/100.00, whose recall is saturated but F1/ACC are far lower). The paper notes most baselines degrade sharply from malicious-user-request detection to prompt-injection detection (e.g., GPT-4o F1 drops from 84.8 on AgentHarm-Traj to 63.03 on ASB-Traj) and struggle to identify harmful tools on ASB-Traj, while TS-Guard stays strong across all four unsafe patterns. In the guarded-agent evaluation (Table 4), with GPT-4o as backbone the plain ReAct agent has ASR 56.16/82.25/80.00 and Refusal 62.50 across AgentDojo/ASB-DPI/ASB-IPI/AgentHarm; ReAct-LlamaFirewall(TS-Guard) drives ASR down to 0.95/5.50/5.50 and Refusal up to 96.59 but Utility on ASB-DPI falls from 12.50 to 2.75; ReAct-TS-Flow(TS-Guard) achieves comparable safety (ASR 1.16/6.76/6.19, Refusal 94.32) while Utility on ASB-DPI rises to 18.87 (above the undefended baseline's 12.50) and AgentDojo Utility rises to 42.78 (above baseline's 26.87) -- i.e., TS-Flow's feedback-driven correction, rather than abort-on-detect, both cuts attacks and preserves/improves benign task completion, which the paper frames as its central empirical claim ('reduces harmful tool invocations by up to 65% on average... improves benign task performance by approximately 10%'). Ablations (Fig. 3) show RL-only training generalizes better than SFT or SFT+RL (SFT reduces output entropy from 0.74 to 0.61, which the authors link to worse downstream RL gains), and multi-task reward beats single-task reward on F1. A richness ablation (Table 5) shows full TS-Guard feedback beats safety-rating-only feedback on ASB-OPI/AgentHarm (ASR 7.00 vs 26.04; Refusal 95.45 vs 79.54).
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions (not related work) are threefold: (1) TS-Bench, described as the first benchmark for step-level (pre-execution) tool invocation safety detection in LLM agents, built from re-annotated trajectories drawn from four existing agent-safety datasets (AgentAlign, AgentHarm, ASB, AgentDojo), labeling each candidate tool-call step as safe/controversial/unsafe plus whether it is linked to a malicious user request or a prompt-injection attack; (2) TS-Guard, a guardrail model trained via GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) reinforcement learning with a multi-task reward that jointly predicts request harmfulness, third-party attack correlation, and the final step-level safety label in one inference pass, producing an interpretable rationale; and (3) TS-Flow, a guardrail-feedback-driven reasoning framework for ReAct-style agents that injects TS-Guard's pre-execution feedback into the agent's context so it can self-correct instead of aborting the task (in contrast to LlamaFirewall's 'detect-and-abort' paradigm).
threat_model: Two categories of tool-invocation risk are considered, both defined by the paper itself: (a) malicious user requests (MUR) that directly induce unsafe tool calls (e.g., asking the agent to order illegal drugs), and (b) prompt injection attacks (direct or indirect, DPI/IPI) that corrupt an otherwise benign trajectory into invoking unintended, harmful tool calls (e.g., an injected instruction causes the agent to email a credit-card PIN to an attacker address). The manifestations are further split into invoking explicitly harmful tools (HT) versus benign tools with risky arguments (BTRA), giving four unsafe patterns (MUR x PI x HT x BTRA) that TS-Bench is built to cover. The defender's goal is pre-execution interception: judging the safety of a candidate action before it is executed, using the interaction history and available tool set as context.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) guardrail feedback is appended to the agent's context as an external signal in a model-agnostic way, but agents may occasionally fail to fully incorporate it, limiting intervention effectiveness; (2) the agent and guardrail model are trained independently with no explicit coordination, risking misalignment between agent reasoning and guardrail judgments -- future work proposes joint/tighter-coupled training. The paper also separately notes guardrail feedback introduces some processing delay but argues it is within an acceptable range (deferred to an appendix not captured in this extract). Not stated by the authors but evident from the tables: TS-Guard's advantage is largest specifically on the ASB and AgentDojo splits where baselines suffer catastrophic F1/recall collapse under prompt injection, and TS-Guard/ShieldAgent-THU share the same Qwen2.5-7B base as part of the entropy-comparison design, which somewhat narrows the generality of the entropy-mechanism claims in Section 6.2 to that architecture pairing.

datasets_used: TS-Bench (own construction, built from AgentAlign, AgentHarm, ASB, AgentDojo trajectories)
quantitative_results: TS-Guard strict-mode detection: AgentHarm-Traj ACC 84.81 / F1 90.16 / Recall 96.95
TS-Guard strict-mode detection: ASB-Traj ACC 94.97 / F1 94.76 / Recall 93.85
TS-Guard strict-mode detection: AgentDojo-Traj ACC 91.72 / F1 86.18 / Recall 89.49
GPT-4o strict-mode detection: AgentHarm-Traj F1 84.80 / Recall 96.19, dropping to ASB-Traj F1 63.03 / Recall 60.11
GPT-4o backbone, ReAct baseline: ASR 56.16 (AgentDojo) / 82.25 (ASB-DPI) / 80.00 (ASB-IPI); Refusal 62.50, Score 23.53 (AgentHarm)
GPT-4o backbone, ReAct-LlamaFirewall(TS-Guard): ASR 0.95 / 5.50 / 5.50; Refusal 96.59, Score 4.28; but ASB-DPI Utility drops to 2.75
GPT-4o backbone, ReAct-TS-Flow(TS-Guard): ASR 1.16 / 6.76 / 6.19; Utility 42.78 (AgentDojo, above 26.87 baseline) / 18.87 (ASB-DPI, above 12.50 baseline) / 49.01 (ASB-IPI); Refusal 94.32, Score 6.03
Feedback richness ablation (ASB-OPI/AgentHarm): full TS-Guard feedback ASR 7.00 / Refusal 95.45 vs safety-rating-only ASR 26.04 / Refusal 79.54
SFT reduces Qwen2.5-7B-IT output entropy from 0.74 to 0.61 versus base model
baselines_compared: LlamaFirewall (GPT-4o-mini or TS-Guard as alignment-check module)
